facts.text: A cheetah can accelerate from 0 to 60 mph in just three seconds, making it the fastest land animal on Earth.
Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump, but they can swim for up to six hours without touching the bottom.
World Wildlife Day is celebrated annually to raise awareness of the world's wild animals and plants.
A group of flamingos is called a 'flamboyance,' while a group of owls is called a 'parliament.'
Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur, which helps them absorb heat from the sun.
Gorillas share about 98.3% of their DNA with humans and can learn sign language to communicate.
The blue whale's heart is so large that a small child could crawl through its arteries.
Wolves can travel up to 30 miles in a single day while hunting, covering vast wilderness territories.
The platypus is one of only five species of egg-laying mammals, known as monotremes.
Komodo dragons have venomous bites that contain toxins causing rapid blood loss and shock in prey.
The San Diego Zoo was one of the first zoos to use open-air, cageless exhibits to house animals.
An octopus has three hearts, blue blood, and can change color in milliseconds to camouflage itself.
The Arctic tern has the longest migration of any animal, traveling roughly 44,000 miles annually.
Hippos secrete a red oily substance that acts as a natural sunscreen and antibiotic for their skin.
Endangered Species Day highlights the importance of protecting threatened and endangered wildlife.
Crows can recognize individual human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them.
A lion's roar can be heard from up to five miles away, making it the loudest of any big cat.
Giant pandas spend about 12 hours a day eating bamboo to consume enough calories to survive.
Dolphins sleep with one eye open — literally, as half their brain stays awake to watch for predators.
The Tasmanian devil has the strongest bite per unit body mass of any living mammal.
Chimpanzees use tools like sticks to fish termites out of mounds, showing advanced problem-solving skills.
The African elephant is the largest living land animal, with males weighing up to 14,000 pounds.
Snow leopards can leap up to 50 feet in a single bound when hunting in mountainous terrain.
Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to keep from drifting apart in the water.
The great wildebeest migration in East Africa involves over 1.5 million animals crossing the Serengeti.
Kangaroos cannot walk backward, which is why the kangaroo appears on the Australian coat of arms.
World Oceans Day promotes ocean conservation and the protection of marine wildlife globally.
A grizzly bear's bite is strong enough to crush a bowling ball with its jaw.
Orangutans are the most solitary of the great apes, with males living alone most of their lives.
The king cobra is the longest venomous snake in the world, reaching lengths of up to 18 feet.
Koalas sleep up to 22 hours per day because their eucalyptus diet provides very little energy.
African wild dogs have a hunting success rate of about 80%, far higher than lions at around 25%.
The Bronx Zoo in New York opened in 1899 and is one of the largest urban zoos in the United States.
Humpback whales sing complex songs that can last for hours and be heard hundreds of miles away.
A giraffe's tongue is about 18 to 20 inches long and is dark purple to protect it from sunburn.
Coyotes have adapted so well to urban environments that they now live in every major U.S. city.
The WWF (World Wildlife Fund) was founded on September 11, 1961, to protect endangered species.
Elephants can recognize themselves in mirrors, a sign of self-awareness shared by very few species.
The Galápagos giant tortoise can live for over 175 years, making it one of the longest-lived vertebrates.
Wombat droppings are cube-shaped, which prevents them from rolling away and marks their territory.
A jaguar's bite is the strongest of all big cats, powerful enough to crack a turtle's shell.
Lemurs are found only on the island of Madagascar and some nearby islands off the coast of Africa.
The IUCN Red List currently classifies over 44,000 species as threatened with extinction.
Manatees, sometimes called sea cows, are gentle herbivores that can eat up to 10% of their body weight daily.
Black bears are excellent climbers and can ascend trees at speeds faster than most humans can run.
World Animal Day is dedicated to improving the welfare and standards for animals around the world.
The peregrine falcon is the fastest animal on Earth, reaching speeds over 240 mph during a hunting stoop.
Bonobos are known as the most peaceful of the great apes and resolve conflicts through social bonding.
Zebras have unique stripe patterns, like human fingerprints — no two zebras are exactly alike.
The inland taipan of Australia has the most toxic venom of any land snake in the world.
Orcas, or killer whales, are actually the largest members of the dolphin family, not true whales.
Red foxes are the most widely distributed wild carnivore, found across the entire Northern Hemisphere.
Crocodiles have the strongest bite ever measured in a living animal, exceeding 3,700 pounds of force.
The London Zoo, opened in 1828, was originally intended for scientific study rather than public entertainment.
Tigers are the largest wild cat species, with Siberian tigers weighing up to 660 pounds.
World Rhino Day raises awareness about all five rhino species and the threats they face.
Honeybees can recognize human faces and remember them for at least two days.
Sun bears have the longest tongues relative to body size of any bear, reaching up to 10 inches.
Elephants mourn their dead and have been observed returning to the bones of deceased family members.
The spectacled bear is the only bear species native to South America.
Cheetahs cannot roar like other big cats; instead, they chirp, purr, and make high-pitched yelps.
An anaconda can swallow prey much larger than its head because its jaw bones are not fused together.
Wolf packs are actually family units, typically led by a breeding pair rather than an 'alpha male.'
The blue whale is the largest animal ever known to have existed, weighing up to 200 tons.
National Panda Day celebrates giant pandas and the conservation efforts to protect them.
Alligators have been around for over 37 million years, surviving relatively unchanged since the age of dinosaurs.
The rhinoceros beetle can lift 850 times its own body weight, making it one of the strongest animals.
Dolphins have names for each other and will respond when called by their specific whistle.
The chameleon can move its eyes independently, looking in two different directions simultaneously.
Mountain gorillas live in groups called troops, typically led by a dominant silverback male.
Kangaroo rats can survive their entire lives without ever drinking water, getting moisture solely from seeds.
The axolotl can regenerate entire limbs, its heart, and even parts of its brain.
World Elephant Day is observed to bring attention to the plight of African and Asian elephants.
Leopards are incredibly strong and can carry prey twice their own weight up into a tree.
A group of rhinos is called a 'crash,' while a group of hippos is called a 'bloat.'
The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a bubble that reaches temperatures of the sun's surface.
African elephants have the longest gestation period of any land animal at approximately 22 months.
The San Diego Zoo Safari Park spans 1,800 acres and houses over 3,500 animals from 400 species.
Cougars hold the Guinness record for the animal with the most names — over 40 including puma, mountain lion, and catamount.
Whale sharks are the largest fish in the ocean, yet they feed almost exclusively on tiny plankton.
The mimic octopus can impersonate over 15 different species, including lionfish, flatfish, and sea snakes.
Pangolins are the most trafficked mammals in the world despite being protected by international law.
International Tiger Day is observed to raise awareness about tiger conservation worldwide.
Sperm whales have the largest brain of any animal, weighing about 17 pounds.
Honey badgers are known for their fearlessness and have been observed fighting off lions and venomous snakes.
The harpy eagle has talons as large as a grizzly bear's claws and can exert over 110 pounds of pressure.
Elephants communicate using infrasonic sounds below the range of human hearing over distances of several miles.
The Philippine eagle is one of the rarest and most powerful birds of prey in the world.
Chimpanzees have been observed waging wars between rival groups lasting years.
Sea turtles return to the exact beach where they were born to lay their own eggs.
The mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a .22 caliber bullet from a standing start.
World Turtle Day encourages people to learn about and protect turtles and tortoises.
Polar bears are classified as marine mammals because they spend most of their lives on Arctic sea ice.
A group of porcupines is called a 'prickle,' while a group of ravens is called an 'unkindness.'
The golden poison frog has enough toxin to kill 10 adult humans, making it the most poisonous vertebrate.
Lowland gorillas use medicinal plants to treat ailments, a behavior observed over decades of field research.
The Tasmanian devil got its name from early European settlers who were frightened by its loud screeches.
Narwhals have a single long tusk that is actually a sensory organ containing millions of nerve endings.
The dung beetle can move balls of dung 1,141 times its own body weight.
World Lion Day is dedicated to raising awareness about the challenges facing lions in the wild.
Sloths are surprisingly strong swimmers and can hold their breath for up to 40 minutes underwater.
The African savanna elephant's ears are shaped like the continent of Africa.
Wolves mate for life and both parents play active roles in raising their pups.
The electric eel can generate up to 860 volts of electricity, enough to stun a horse.
Koalas have fingerprints so similar to human fingerprints they can confuse crime scene investigators.
The komodo dragon can detect carrion from up to 6 miles away using its forked tongue.
Humpback whales cooperate to create bubble nets that trap schools of fish for group feeding.
The quokka, native to Western Australia, is often called the happiest animal on Earth because of its smile.
World Gorilla Day celebrates gorillas and promotes their conservation in Central Africa.
A saltwater crocodile can go for over a year without eating by slowing its metabolism drastically.
Giraffes only need about 30 minutes of sleep per day, taken in short naps of a few minutes at a time.
The cassowary is considered the most dangerous bird in the world due to its powerful kick and dagger-like claw.
Orangutans build a new sleeping nest in the trees every single night from branches and foliage.
Gray wolves were successfully reintroduced to Yellowstone National Park in 1995 after a 70-year absence.
The star-nosed mole can identify and eat food in as little as 120 milliseconds, making it the fastest-eating mammal.
Bottlenose dolphins have been observed using sponges on their snouts to protect themselves while foraging on the seafloor.
The bald eagle was removed from the U.S. Endangered Species List in 2007 after a remarkable recovery.
World Whale Day is celebrated to honor humpback whales that migrate through Hawaiian waters each winter.
A flamingo can only eat with its head upside down due to the unique structure of its beak.
The snow leopard is so elusive it has earned the nickname 'ghost of the mountains.'
Meerkats take turns standing guard while the rest of the group forages for food.
The Amur leopard is the rarest big cat in the world, with fewer than 100 remaining in the wild.
Elephants can distinguish between human languages and identify whether a speaker is a threat.
Wombats can run at speeds up to 25 mph in short bursts when they feel threatened.
The vampire bat is the only mammal that feeds entirely on blood.
Sea otters have the densest fur of any mammal, with up to one million hairs per square inch.
World Penguin Day marks the annual northern migration of Adélie penguins in Antarctica.
A tiger's stripes are unique to each individual, much like human fingerprints.
The leafy sea dragon is so well camouflaged it is nearly invisible among seaweed.
Cheetahs have semi-retractable claws that act like cleats, giving them extra grip when sprinting.
The California condor was brought back from the brink of extinction through captive breeding; only 22 remained in 1987.
Capuchin monkeys have been observed using rocks as hammers and anvils to crack open nuts.
A hippopotamus can open its mouth nearly 180 degrees, wide enough to fit a four-foot-tall child inside.
The pangolin rolls into a tight ball when threatened, and its name comes from the Malay word for 'roller.'
Albatrosses can glide for hours without flapping their wings by using dynamic soaring techniques.
World Snake Day is observed to raise awareness and appreciation for all snake species.
African wild dogs vote on group decisions by sneezing — the more sneezes, the more likely the group will move.
The great white shark can detect one drop of blood in 25 gallons of water.
Silverback gorillas can weigh up to 430 pounds and are about 10 times stronger than the average human.
The bowhead whale has the longest lifespan of any mammal, with some individuals living over 200 years.
Red pandas are more closely related to raccoons than to giant pandas despite sharing a name.
Crocodiles swallow stones to help them dive deeper, as the stones act as ballast.
The okapi, found only in the Democratic Republic of Congo, is the giraffe's closest living relative.
Chimpanzees share about 98.7% of their DNA with humans, making them our closest living relatives.
The Galápagos Islands are home to species found nowhere else on Earth, inspiring Darwin's theory of evolution.
World Bee Day highlights the importance of bees and other pollinators to global ecosystems.
A group of jellyfish is called a 'smack,' and some species are bioluminescent.
Beavers are second only to humans in their ability to manipulate their environment by building dams.
The tiger population in India has grown from 1,411 in 2006 to over 3,600 thanks to conservation efforts.
Emperor penguins can dive to depths of over 1,800 feet, deeper than any other bird.
Leopard geckos can detach their tails when grabbed by predators and later regrow them.
The kakapo is the world's only flightless parrot and also one of the longest-living birds.
Manatees are related to elephants and share a distant common ancestor from about 60 million years ago.
The wolverine, despite weighing only about 40 pounds, can drive grizzly bears away from food.
World Migratory Bird Day is celebrated to highlight the importance of migratory bird conservation.
A woodpecker's tongue wraps around its skull to cushion its brain during rapid pecking at up to 20 times per second.
The sailfish is the fastest fish in the ocean, swimming at speeds up to 68 mph.
Wolves howl to communicate with pack members over distances of up to 10 miles.
The Sumatran rhino is the smallest and most endangered of all rhino species, with fewer than 80 remaining.
Cuttlefish have W-shaped pupils and can see polarized light invisible to the human eye.
The African bush elephant can consume over 300 pounds of vegetation in a single day.
Pufferfish contain tetrodotoxin, a substance 1,200 times more poisonous than cyanide.
Cheetah cubs have a mane of long fur on their backs that mimics the honey badger to deter predators.
International Polar Bear Day raises awareness about the impact of climate change on polar bear habitat.
The colossal squid has the largest eyes in the animal kingdom, each about the size of a dinner plate.
Elephants use their trunks to breathe, drink, grab food, and even snorkel across deep water.
Lyrebirds can mimic almost any sound they hear, including chainsaws, car alarms, and camera shutters.
The naked mole rat is virtually immune to cancer and can survive up to 18 minutes without oxygen.
Giraffes have the same number of neck vertebrae as humans — just seven, but each is over 10 inches long.
The secretary bird stomps on snakes with a force five times its own body weight to kill them.
Bengal tigers are strong swimmers and often kill prey while swimming in rivers and mangrove swamps.
World Habitat Day promotes awareness of the need for adequate shelter for wildlife and humans.
Otters have a pocket of loose skin under their arms where they store their favorite rocks for cracking open shells.
The proboscis monkey has an unusually large nose that amplifies its vocalizations to attract mates.
The Yangtze River dolphin, or baiji, was declared functionally extinct in 2006.
Horseshoe crabs have blue blood that is used to test the safety of vaccines and medical devices.
A flea can jump 150 times its own body length, equivalent to a human jumping over a 75-story building.
The giant Pacific octopus can squeeze through any opening larger than its beak, the only hard part of its body.
Flamingos are born white or gray and turn pink from the carotenoid pigments in their diet of brine shrimp.
World Monkey Day celebrates all primates and raises awareness about their conservation.
Arctic foxes can survive temperatures as low as minus 58°F thanks to their incredibly dense fur.
Male seahorses are the ones that carry and give birth to their young, unique among the animal kingdom.
The clouded leopard has the longest canine teeth relative to body size of any living cat.
Elephants can hear through their feet, detecting vibrations in the ground from miles away.
The Iberian lynx was once the most endangered cat species, but conservation raised its numbers from under 100 to over 1,600.
Bats are the only mammals capable of true sustained flight.
A blue whale's tongue alone can weigh as much as an elephant.
The dhole, or Asian wild dog, communicates using whistles rather than howls or barks.
Galapagos marine iguanas are the only lizards in the world that forage for food in the ocean.
World Giraffe Day celebrates the tallest living terrestrial animal on the longest day of the year.
A housefly hums in the key of F and beats its wings about 200 times per second.
Penguins propose to their mates with a pebble, and bonded pairs often return to each other year after year.
The thorny devil lizard of Australia can drink water through its skin by channeling moisture toward its mouth.
New Caledonian crows can create compound tools by combining two or more components.
The world's largest land carnivore is the polar bear, with males weighing up to 1,500 pounds.
Pangolins can consume up to 70 million ants and termites per year.
The resplendent quetzal was considered divine by ancient Maya and Aztec civilizations.
International Day for Biological Diversity highlights the importance of biodiversity conservation.
Elephants are afraid of bees, and farmers in Africa use beehive fences to keep elephants out of crops.
A chameleon's tongue can be twice the length of its body and strikes prey in less than 0.07 seconds.
The Svalbard Global Seed Vault stores seeds to protect against the loss of plant diversity, indirectly safeguarding animal habitats.
Slow lorises are the only venomous primates, producing toxin from glands on their elbows.
Ostriches have the largest eyes of any land animal, each nearly two inches in diameter.
The vaquita is the most endangered marine mammal, with fewer than 10 individuals believed to remain.
Spotted hyenas live in matriarchal societies where females outrank all males in the clan.
Porcupines have about 30,000 quills that are actually modified hairs coated in keratin.
National Endangered Species Day was established in the United States in 2006.
The coconut crab is the largest land-dwelling arthropod, with a leg span of up to three feet.
A rhinoceros horn is made of keratin, the same protein found in human hair and fingernails.
The wandering albatross has the largest wingspan of any living bird, reaching over 11 feet.
Parrotfish produce up to 840 pounds of sand per year by eating and excreting coral.
Jaguars are the only big cats that kill prey by biting directly through the skull.
The snow monkey, or Japanese macaque, bathes in hot springs to stay warm during cold winters.
Tardigrades, also called water bears, can survive in the vacuum of space and extreme radiation.
World Biodiversity Day promotes understanding of biodiversity issues across the globe.
The fennec fox has enormous ears relative to its body, which help dissipate heat in the Sahara Desert.
Hummingbirds can fly backward and are the only birds that can sustain hovering flight.
The glass frog has translucent skin on its belly, allowing you to see its beating heart and organs.
Mountain lions have the largest range of any wild land mammal in the Americas, from Canada to Patagonia.
Dolphins can stay awake for 15 days straight by alternating which half of their brain sleeps.
The Goliath birdeater tarantula is the world's largest spider by mass, weighing up to 6 ounces.
Cleaner wrasses pass the mirror test, suggesting self-awareness in fish.
African elephants have about 40,000 muscles in their trunk alone.
National Bison Day honors the American bison, once hunted to near extinction with only 541 remaining in 1889.
The immortal jellyfish can revert to its juvenile polyp stage after reaching maturity, potentially living forever.
Snow leopards wrap their thick tails around their bodies and faces to stay warm while sleeping.
Vampire bats share blood meals with hungry roost-mates, demonstrating reciprocal altruism.
The horned lizard can squirt blood from its eyes up to five feet as a defense mechanism.
Wolves were the first animals to be domesticated by humans, roughly 15,000 to 40,000 years ago.
The aye-aye uses its long middle finger to tap on trees and find grubs by echolocation.
Sperm whales sleep vertically in groups, bobbing near the surface of the ocean.
World Chimpanzee Day celebrates and raises awareness about chimpanzees and their conservation.
The African wild dog is one of the most endangered mammals in Africa, with fewer than 6,600 remaining.
Electric eels are not true eels but are more closely related to catfish and carp.
The Andean condor has the largest wing area of any bird, at roughly 75 square feet spread.
Prairie dogs greet each other with kisses — they touch their front teeth together to identify group members.
A group of crows is called a 'murder,' and they have been known to hold funerals for their dead.
The kiwi bird has nostrils at the tip of its beak, unlike any other bird species.
Giant tortoises in the Galápagos helped Darwin develop his theory of natural selection.
The Amur tiger is the largest subspecies of tiger and the biggest cat in the world.
Manta rays have the largest brain-to-body ratio of any cold-blooded fish.
World Otter Day promotes the conservation of all 13 species of otter worldwide.
Coral reefs support about 25% of all marine species despite covering less than 1% of the ocean floor.
A mosquito has 47 teeth, though they are so small they are technically called 'teeth-like' structures.
The binturong, or bearcat, smells like buttered popcorn due to a chemical in its urine.
Dolphins have been trained by militaries to detect underwater mines using their echolocation abilities.
The California condor's wingspan can reach nearly 10 feet, making it the largest flying bird in North America.
Orangutans share 97% of their DNA with humans and can learn to use tools and solve problems.
Baby elephants suck their trunks for comfort, similar to how human babies suck their thumbs.
World Tapir Day is dedicated to raising awareness about the four species of tapir.
The bombardier beetle defends itself by spraying boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen.
Gray whales make one of the longest migrations of any mammal, traveling 12,000 miles round trip annually.
African grey parrots can learn over 1,000 words and use them in contextually appropriate ways.
The numbat is one of only two marsupials that are active primarily during the day.
A grizzly bear can smell food from up to 18 miles away.
The Mexican free-tailed bat is the fastest mammal in level flight, reaching speeds over 99 mph.
Crows have been observed sliding down snowy rooftops repeatedly, apparently just for fun.
The blue-ringed octopus is small enough to fit in your palm but carries enough venom to kill 26 adults.
The WWF's panda logo was inspired by Chi Chi, a giant panda at the London Zoo in the 1960s.
World Habitat Conservation Day emphasizes the preservation of critical wildlife ecosystems.
Giraffes only sleep about 30 minutes per day, and they often sleep standing up.
The tuatara of New Zealand is the only surviving member of an order of reptiles that thrived 200 million years ago.
Gorillas build new nests every night and occasionally take daytime naps in hastily constructed day nests.
Fireflies produce light through a chemical reaction called bioluminescence in their abdomens.
The Nile crocodile can hold its breath underwater for up to two hours while waiting to ambush prey.
Pikas, small mountain-dwelling mammals, create haystacks of dried plants to survive winter.
Male lions sleep up to 20 hours per day, while females do most of the hunting.
World Dolphin Day raises awareness about the threats dolphins face from pollution and fishing nets.
The archerfish can spit a jet of water up to five feet to knock insects off branches above the water.
Vervet monkeys have distinct alarm calls for different predators — leopards, eagles, and snakes each trigger unique calls.
The gharial is a critically endangered crocodilian with fewer than 650 adults remaining in the wild.
Seahorses have no stomach and must eat almost constantly because food passes through them so quickly.
The capybara is the world's largest rodent, weighing up to 140 pounds.
Sea cucumbers breathe through their rear end and can expel their internal organs to escape predators.
Raccoons can remember solutions to tasks for up to three years after learning them.
The addax antelope is one of the rarest mammals on Earth, with fewer than 100 remaining in the Sahara.
World Shark Awareness Day aims to dispel myths and raise awareness about the importance of sharks.
Elephants can recognize old friends and display excitement when reunited after years apart.
The king vulture is revered in Mayan mythology and can smell carrion from miles away.
Dragonflies have a prey-capture success rate of 95%, making them the most efficient hunters in nature.
Panda bears have an extra 'thumb' — actually an enlarged wrist bone — that helps them grip bamboo.
The quahog clam named Ming was found to be 507 years old, making it the oldest known individual animal.
Snow leopards cannot roar; instead, they produce a unique sound called a 'chuff' to greet each other.
The mandrill is the largest species of monkey, with males weighing up to 77 pounds.
World Frog Day raises awareness about amphibian conservation and declining frog populations.
Elephants are one of only a few species known to have rituals around death, gently touching the bones of the deceased.
The giant squid remained unseen alive until 2004, when Japanese researchers photographed one at depth.
Flamingos can live for over 70 years in captivity, making them one of the longest-lived birds.
Wolverines can smell prey buried under 20 feet of snow and dig them out with their powerful claws.
The red-eyed tree frog uses its vivid colors to startle predators, a defense known as startle coloration.
A group of pugs is called a 'grumble,' while a group of hedgehogs is called an 'array.'
The harpy eagle's talons are strong enough to crush bones and snatch monkeys and sloths from treetops.
Coral is not a plant — it is a colony of tiny animals called polyps that build limestone skeletons.
International Day of Forests highlights how forests are critical habitat for 80% of terrestrial species.
The blobfish looks like a normal fish at depth but appears gelatinous at surface pressure due to its low-density flesh.
Whale sharks can live for over 100 years and don't reach sexual maturity until around age 30.
Painted dogs are among the most efficient predators in Africa, with hunt success rates exceeding 80%.
A newborn kangaroo, called a joey, is about the size of a grape when it crawls into its mother's pouch.
The saiga antelope has an unusually bulbous nose that filters dust in summer and warms frigid air in winter.
Vampire squid live in the deep ocean and feed on drifting organic particles called marine snow.
The Tasmanian tiger, or thylacine, was declared extinct in 1936 though reported sightings still occur.
World Ranger Day honors park rangers who protect wildlife and natural areas worldwide.
Cassowaries can run at speeds up to 31 mph through dense tropical forest.
The lyrebird's ability to mimic sounds is so accurate it can fool other bird species.
Bottlenose dolphins can jump up to 20 feet out of the water.
The kakapo is the heaviest parrot in the world, weighing up to 9 pounds, and can live over 90 years.
African elephants use different vocalizations to refer to specific individuals in their herd.
The shoebill stork stands perfectly still for hours, then strikes with lightning speed to catch lungfish.
Pistol shrimp create shockwaves with their claws that can stun or kill small fish.
The Indian gharial can have over 100 teeth at any given time, more than any other crocodilian.
Migrating monarch butterflies travel up to 3,000 miles from Canada to Mexico each fall.
World Crocodile Day raises awareness about the 23 species of crocodilians and their conservation.
Cheetahs need to rest for at least 30 minutes after a sprint before they can eat, leaving their prey vulnerable to theft.
The Greenland shark is the longest-lived vertebrate, with some individuals estimated to be over 400 years old.
Elephants can differentiate between the languages and voices of different human ethnic groups.
The glass sponge can live for over 10,000 years, making it one of the oldest living organisms.
Ravens can plan for the future by saving tools and bartering tokens for food rewards.
The red panda was discovered 48 years before the giant panda and was the original 'panda.'
Komodo dragons can eat up to 80% of their body weight in a single meal.
Blue whales produce the loudest sound of any animal, reaching 188 decibels and traveling hundreds of miles.
International Snow Leopard Day supports conservation of snow leopards across Central Asia.
Adelie penguins collect pebbles to build nests and sometimes steal pebbles from neighboring nests.
The thorny dragon can drink water by standing in a puddle — capillary channels in its skin draw water to its mouth.
A cougar can jump vertically up to 18 feet from a standing position.
The pangolin is covered in overlapping scales made of keratin, providing armor against predators.
Walruses use their tusks to haul themselves out of the water, which is how they got their scientific name, Odobenus, meaning 'one who walks with teeth.'
The proboscis monkey's large nose inflates like a balloon when it makes warning calls.
Honey badgers have been observed using tools, such as logs, to climb over fences.
The zoo at Basel, Switzerland, was the first to successfully breed an Indian rhinoceros in captivity.
World Pangolin Day is observed to raise awareness about the world's most trafficked mammal.
Leopard seals are one of Antarctica's top predators and are known to patrol penguin colonies.
A snail can sleep for up to three years during periods of drought or extreme cold.
The orchid mantis mimics flower petals so convincingly that pollinating insects land directly on it.
Elephants have been observed consoling distressed members of their group with gentle trunk touches and vocalizations.
The fishing cat of Southeast Asia is one of the few cats that regularly catches fish by diving into water.
The gerenuk, or giraffe gazelle, can stand on its hind legs to browse on tall bushes.
African forest elephants are a distinct species from savanna elephants and play a crucial role in seed dispersal.
World Biodiversity Awareness Day promotes understanding of the variety of life on Earth.
The pistol shrimp's snapping claw produces a flash of light called sonoluminescence.
Wolverines are known to travel over 15 miles per day in search of food in harsh northern climates.
Cassowaries swallow fruit whole and are critical seed dispersers for over 100 plant species in tropical forests.
The world's smallest mammal is the bumblebee bat of Thailand, weighing less than a penny.
Beluga whales are called 'canaries of the sea' because of the wide variety of sounds they produce.
Secretary birds stamp on the ground with a force up to five times their body weight to kill snakes.
The white rhinoceros is not actually white — 'white' is a mistranslation of the Dutch word 'wijd' meaning wide.
Cuttlefish can count and will choose a higher number of prey items when given the option.
The northern quoll of Australia is declining rapidly due to poisoning from invasive cane toads.
World Orangutan Day promotes conservation efforts for orangutans in Borneo and Sumatra.
The okapi was not known to Western science until 1901, despite being well known to local Congolese people.
A grizzly bear can run at speeds up to 35 mph, fast enough to outrun a horse over short distances.
The axolotl is critically endangered in the wild, found only in a few canals near Mexico City.
Maned wolves are neither wolves nor foxes — they are the only species in their genus Chrysocyon.
The Sumatran orangutan is one of the rarest great apes, with fewer than 14,000 remaining in the wild.
Hippos kill more people in Africa each year than lions, leopards, and crocodiles combined.
The alpine ibex can scale near-vertical dam walls to lick mineral salts from the concrete.
Bowerbirds build elaborate decorated structures to attract mates, arranging objects by color and size.
The ocelot's name comes from the Aztec word 'tlalocelot,' meaning field tiger.
A woodpecker can peck up to 20 times per second without suffering brain damage thanks to a spongy skull structure.
The markhor, Pakistan's national animal, has corkscrew horns that can grow over five feet long.
Manta rays visit cleaning stations on reefs where small fish remove parasites from their skin.
The European bison, or wisent, was saved from extinction through captive breeding from just 12 surviving individuals.
Pygmy marmosets are the smallest monkeys in the world, weighing only about 3.5 ounces.
The fossa is the top predator in Madagascar and is the largest carnivore on the island.
Sawfish use their rostrum (saw-like snout) to slash through schools of fish, stunning or impaling them.
Mudskippers are fish that can walk on land, climb trees, and breathe through their skin.
The gemsbok can survive in the Namib Desert without drinking water for weeks by extracting moisture from the plants it eats.
Coconut crabs can crack open coconuts with their claws and have a grip strength comparable to a lion's bite.
The silky anteater is the smallest anteater species, weighing less than a pound.
Conservationists use trained dogs to sniff out poached ivory, rhino horn, and endangered animal products.
The Chinese giant salamander is the largest amphibian in the world, reaching lengths over five feet.
Mongooses are one of the few animals immune to certain snake venoms due to specialized acetylcholine receptors.
The snowy owl can turn its head 270 degrees to scan for prey across the Arctic tundra.
Wildebeest calves can stand and run within minutes of being born to keep up with the migrating herd.
The golden eagle can dive at speeds exceeding 150 mph to strike prey with devastating force.
Elephants have been known to travel long distances to visit the remains of deceased family members years after their death.
The kea, a New Zealand parrot, is known for its intelligence and has been filmed solving multi-step puzzles.
The Siberian tiger can leap over 25 feet in a single bound when pouncing on prey.
River otters can close their ears and nostrils underwater and can hold their breath for up to eight minutes.
The sunfish, or mola mola, is the heaviest bony fish in the world, weighing up to 5,000 pounds.
Wild chimpanzees have been documented self-medicating by eating specific plants when they are ill.
The red wolf is one of the most endangered canids in the world, with only about 20 remaining in the wild.
Komodo dragons occasionally practice parthenogenesis, reproducing without mating.
The sand cat is the only wild cat that lives primarily in true desert environments.
Sea lions can rotate their hind flippers forward to walk on land, unlike true seals.
Giraffes have been observed humming to each other at night at frequencies too low for humans to easily hear.
The pangolin's tongue can extend up to 16 inches and is anchored near the pelvis rather than the throat.
Tasmanian devils store fat in their tails, which swell when the animal is healthy and well-fed.
